items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Use the word "immediately" only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 2-1-5a.

items.instruction: Which term is specifically paired with prompt compliance before the situation becomes imminent?
items.question_text: Under 2-1-5, which word is specifically used when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-5b, use the word "expedite" only when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: If time permits, provide the reason for the action.
items.explanation: That instruction appears in 2-1-5c.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 2-1-5, The terms "immediately" and "expedite" are used for different levels of urgency as a situation moves toward becoming imminent.
items.explanation: The paragraph distinguishes between the urgency conveyed by "immediately" and by "expedite."

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Traffic is developing quickly and you need a climbing aircraft to comply at once to avoid an imminent conflict. A trainee suggests using the word "expedite" because it sounds less forceful than "immediately."
items.para_context: Under 2-1-5, use "immediately" only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation. Use "expedite" only when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation. If time permits, include the reason.

choices.text: Use "immediately" because compliance is required now to avoid an imminent situation, and include the reason if time permits.
Use "expedite" because it means the same thing as "immediately" in every case.
Avoid either term because controllers should never add urgency words to altitude instructions.
Issue only the new altitude and wait to see whether the pilot climbs fast enough before adding urgency language.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-5, "immediately" is reserved for avoiding an imminent situation, while "expedite" is used when prompt compliance is needed to avoid the development of one.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Provide airport traffic control service based only upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions.
items.explanation: That directive is the body of 3-1-1.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When operating in accordance with CFRs, the pilot is responsible to avoid collision with other aircraft.
items.explanation: That responsibility statement appears in the note to 3-1-1.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A trainee says tower service should be based on what pilots report seeing, and that traffic information is unnecessary because pilots are responsible for collision avoidance under the CFRs.
items.para_context: Under 3-1-1, provide airport traffic control service based only upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions. The note adds that although pilots remain responsible for collision avoidance under the CFRs, traffic information can aid pilots near terminal locations.

choices.text: Base control service on observed or known traffic and airport conditions, and provide traffic information when it can aid pilots near terminal locations.
Base control service on pilot reports alone because they are responsible for seeing and avoiding other traffic.
Withhold traffic information in terminal areas because the CFRs make it unnecessary.
Provide airport traffic control service according to whichever aircraft reports the most restrictive conditions.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-1, airport traffic control service is based on observed or known traffic and airport conditions, while traffic information can still aid pilots near terminal locations.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Use approved ATC surveillance sources.
items.explanation: That instruction opens 5-1-2.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Outside Class A airspace, secondary radar may be used as the sole display source only under the listed conditions.
items.explanation: That scope statement summarizes 5-1-2a2.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Do not use secondary radar only to conduct surveillance (ASR) final approaches unless an emergency exists and the pilot concurs.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 5-1-2b.

items.instruction: Does this minima or separation statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Targets derived from WAM cannot be used to provide 3 NM separation in the EAS.
items.explanation: That note appears in 5-1-2c.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-2-13, Inoperative or Malfunctioning Interrogator.
items.explanation: That citation appears in 5-1-2.

items.instruction: Which condition is specifically listed as allowing secondary radar to be the sole display source outside Class A airspace?
items.question_text: Under 5-1-2, which condition is specifically listed as allowing secondary radar to be used as the sole display source outside Class A airspace?

choices.text: The primary radar is temporarily unusable or out of service.
Traffic volume is low enough that primary radar is not needed.
A pilot requests radar vectors using only transponder information.
The facility has suspended all published instrument procedures for the area.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-2a2, temporary primary-radar outage is one of the listed conditions.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: At a terminal facility outside Class A airspace, the primary radar is temporarily unusable and only secondary radar coverage remains available for a portion of the service area.
items.para_context: Secondary radar may be used as the sole display source outside Class A only when additional coverage exists beyond primary radar coverage or when the primary radar is temporarily unusable or out of service. When those conditions exist, advise pilots that primary radar is unavailable and radar services are available only to transponder or ADS-B equipped aircraft, unless the advisory is already provided by ATIS or other appropriate notice.

choices.text: Advise pilots that primary radar is unavailable and that radar services are available only to transponder or ADS-B equipped aircraft, unless the advisory has already been provided by ATIS or other appropriate notice.
Continue normal primary-radar services without any advisory because secondary radar alone is always interchangeable with primary radar in terminal airspace.
Conduct ASR final approaches normally using secondary radar only, even without an emergency or pilot concurrence.
Suspend all radar services to every aircraft until primary radar is restored, regardless of secondary radar coverage.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-2, secondary radar-only operations outside Class A are limited to specified conditions, and the required advisory must be issued unless already covered by ATIS or other appropriate notice.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Refer all EA activity requests to the appropriate center supervisor.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 5-1-3a.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7610.4, Chapter 2, Section 3, Electronic Attack (EA) Mission Coordination.
items.explanation: That citation appears in 5-1-3.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: In terminal operations, suspension of EA activity is requested through the ARTCC, and direct emergency-frequency broadcast is authorized only when immediate cessation is required.
items.explanation: That scope statement summarizes 5-1-3b2.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Each stop request must include the facility name, type of EA activity, radar band affected, and, when feasible, the expected duration of suspension.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-1-3d.

items.instruction: Which item is specifically included in each EA stop request?
items.question_text: Under 5-1-3, which item is specifically included in each stop request?

choices.text: The runway configuration at the nearest airport.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-3d, the radar band affected is one of the items included in each stop request.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You are working a terminal radar position, and ongoing electronic attack activity is interfering with the operational use of radar. The interference must stop immediately.
items.para_context: In terminal operations, request suspension of EA activity through the ARTCC. If immediate cessation is required, broadcast the stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC of the direct broadcast as soon as possible.

choices.text: Request suspension through the ARTCC, and if immediate cessation is required, broadcast directly on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC as soon as possible.
Contact the aircraft directly on the working frequency and let the ARTCC discover the stop request later.
Continue operations without action because EA requests are handled only by the military unit once approved.
Suspend the narrowband radar first and wait for the Systems Engineer before making any stop request to the EA aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-3, terminal stop requests go through the ARTCC, but immediate cessation may be requested directly on the emergency frequency with prompt ARTCC notification.

items.instruction: Which event is specifically listed as requiring validation of a Mode C altitude readout?
items.question_text: Under 5-2-15, which event is specifically listed as requiring validation of a Mode C altitude readout?

choices.text: Track start from coast or frozen status.
Any routine change in beacon code with no other indication of a problem.
Only after the aircraft levels off above FL 180.
Only after the pilot requests altitude verification.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-15a2, validation is required after track start from coast or frozen status.

items.instruction: Does this minima or tolerance statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: An altitude readout is valid when it varies less than 300 feet from the pilot reported altitude.
items.explanation: That validation standard appears in 5-2-15b1.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-2-21, Altitude Filters.
items.explanation: That citation appears in 5-2-15.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When unable to validate the readout, do not use the Mode C altitude information for separation.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-2-15c.

items.instruction: The controller is validating an invalid Mode C readout below FL180. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: MADISON ALTIMETER THREE ZERO ONE TWO, VERIFY ALTITUDE.

choices.text: Madison altimeter three zero one two, verify altitude, Citation Two One Charlie.
Madison altimeter two niner niner two, verify altitude, Citation Two One Charlie.
Madison altimeter three zero one two, verify flight level, Citation Two One Charlie.
Stop altitude squawk, Citation Two One Charlie.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-15, when an aircraft below FL180 shows an invalid Mode C readout, issue the correct altimeter setting and verify the altitude.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You observe an aircraft below FL180 with an invalid Mode C readout. After issuing the correct altimeter and confirming the pilot's reported altitude, the readout remains invalid.
items.para_context: When the invalid Mode C readout continues below FL180, instruct the pilot to turn off the altitude-reporting part of the transponder and include the reason, and notify the operations supervisor-in-charge of the aircraft call sign. Do not use unvalidated Mode C for separation.

choices.text: Instruct the pilot to stop altitude squawk with the reason and notify the operations supervisor-in-charge of the aircraft call sign.
Continue using the invalid Mode C readout for separation because the pilot verbally confirmed the altitude.
Ignore the problem unless the aircraft climbs above FL180.
Terminate the flight plan because any invalid Mode C readout requires immediate cancellation of radar service.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-15, once the invalid readout persists, altitude squawk is stopped, the supervisor is notified, and the invalid Mode C is not used for separation.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When the data block is displayed and visible and one of the listed identification conditions exists, you may consider an auto-acquired aircraft identified.
items.explanation: That rule summarizes 5-3-4a.

items.instruction: Which data block code is specifically listed as preventing automated-handoff identification under this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 5-3-4, which data block code is specifically listed as preventing the NAS automated handoff condition from qualifying the target as identified?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-4a2, CST is one of the listed data block indications that disqualify the automated handoff condition.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: A displaced data block must be updated at all times.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-3-4c.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 3-1-9, Use of Tower Radar Displays.
items.explanation: That citation appears in 5-3-4.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A terminal auto-acquired aircraft has a visible data block. The tagged target identity has already been confirmed through approved radar or beacon identification procedures, and the data block is properly aligned with the target.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-4, consider an auto-acquired aircraft identified when the data block is displayed and visible and either approved radar or beacon identification procedures confirmed the tagged target, or a qualifying NAS automated handoff exists. Maintain target identity with the data block unless it is in coast status or displaced, and keep displaced data blocks updated at all times.

choices.text: Consider the aircraft identified and use the data block to maintain target identity while it remains properly aligned and not in coast status.
Refuse to use the data block because terminal automation identification always requires a fresh IDENT after auto-acquisition.
Wait until the aircraft leaves terminal airspace because auto-acquired targets cannot be identified until en route handoff.
Ignore any future displacement because once the tag is identified it never needs updating.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-4, a visible auto-acquired data block may establish identification under the listed conditions, and the data block is then used to maintain identity unless it is coasted or displaced.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Do not use Mode C to effect vertical separation with an aircraft on a cruise clearance, contact approach, or as specified in paragraph 5-14-4, subparagraph f3.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 5-5-6a.

items.instruction: Which condition is specifically listed as requiring you to wait before reassigning the altitude?
items.question_text: Under 5-5-6, which condition is specifically listed as requiring you to wait until the first aircraft is observed at or passing another altitude separated by the appropriate minima before assigning its former altitude to another aircraft?

choices.text: Aircraft are conducting military aerial refueling.
Aircraft are flying a standard instrument departure with no turbulence report.
The aircraft has accepted a speed adjustment for in-trail spacing.
The aircraft is established on a visual approach with the airport in sight.

items.explanation: Military aerial refueling is one of the listed conditions in 5-5-6b.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 7-4-7, Contact Approach.
items.explanation: That citation appears in 5-5-6.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Assign an altitude to an aircraft only after the aircraft previously at that altitude is observed at or passing through another altitude separated from the first by the appropriate minima when severe turbulence is reported, aircraft are conducting military aerial refueling, or the first aircraft has been issued a climb or descent at pilot's discretion.
items.explanation: That requirement summarizes 5-5-6b.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft previously at FL230 has been issued a pilot's discretion descent, and you want to assign FL230 to another aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-6b3, when the aircraft previously at that altitude has been issued a climb or descent at pilot's discretion, assign the altitude to another aircraft only after the first aircraft is observed at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minima.

choices.text: Wait until the first aircraft is observed at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minima before assigning FL230 to the second aircraft.
Assign FL230 immediately because pilot's discretion descent satisfies the same rule as an ordinary observed climb or descent.
Use Mode C alone to reassign FL230 even if the position symbol is lagging and the aircraft has not reached another separated altitude.
Apply the same procedure only if the first aircraft is on a cruise clearance, not on pilot's discretion descent.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-6b3, pilot's discretion climb or descent is one of the exceptions requiring the first aircraft to be observed at or passing another appropriately separated altitude before the old altitude is reassigned.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: When vectoring a departing aircraft on a radar SID, concurrent use of a diverse vector area (DVA) is not permitted.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 5-8-1a.

items.instruction: Which instruction is specifically authorized when the radar SID route description includes a published heading range?
items.question_text: Under 5-8-1, which instruction is specifically authorized when the radar SID route description says "Fly assigned heading," "as assigned by ATC," or similar language with a published range of headings?

choices.text: Assign headings or vectors as needed without exceeding the published heading range until reaching the MVA or MIA.
Assign any heading needed for traffic without regard to the published heading range until radar contact is terminated.
Suspend the SID and use the DVA concurrently to issue any heading the pilot accepts.
Delay all headings until the aircraft is above the MVA or MIA and then resume the published route automatically.

items.explanation: Per 5-8-1b, assigned headings or vectors may not exceed the published heading range until reaching the MVA or MIA.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-6-3, Vectors Below Minimum Altitude.
items.explanation: That citation appears in 5-8-1.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When the departure route description on a radar SID contains "Fly assigned heading," "as assigned by ATC," or similar phrases with a published range of headings, assign headings or vectors as needed not to exceed those headings in the published range until reaching the MVA or MIA.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-8-1b.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You are vectoring a departure on a radar SID. Another controller proposes using a diverse vector area at the same time because it seems easier to manage obstacle clearance.
items.para_context: Under 5-8-1, concurrent use of a diverse vector area is not permitted when vectoring a departing aircraft on a radar SID. If the route description contains assigned-heading language with a published range, headings or vectors may be assigned only within that range until reaching the MVA or MIA.

choices.text: Do not use the diverse vector area concurrently with the radar SID, and keep any assigned headings within the published range until reaching the MVA or MIA.
Use the diverse vector area concurrently as long as the departure remains in radar contact.
Suspend the radar SID automatically whenever obstacle clearance seems easier under the DVA.
Assign any heading needed for traffic because the published heading range on the SID is advisory only.

items.explanation: Per 5-8-1, DVA use is not concurrent with radar SID vectoring, and assigned headings must stay within the published range until the MVA or MIA is reached.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Do not use coast tracks in the application of either radar or nonradar separation criteria.
items.explanation: That is the full text of 5-13-7.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Coast tracks are not used in the application of radar or nonradar separation criteria.
items.explanation: That statement restates the requirement in 5-13-7.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A target has coasted and another controller suggests continuing to use the coast track to maintain separation until the live target updates again.
items.para_context: Under 5-13-7, coast tracks are not used in the application of either radar or nonradar separation criteria.

choices.text: Do not use the coast track to apply radar or nonradar separation criteria.
Use the coast track for nonradar separation only, because the restriction applies only to radar separation.
Use the coast track temporarily whenever no other controller objects.
Use the coast track if the pilot confirms the last reported position over the frequency.

items.explanation: Per 5-13-7, coast tracks are not used for either radar or nonradar separation criteria.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Use mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima only when direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.
items.explanation: That is the full text of 6-1-1.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are not maintained, do not use mileage-based procedures and minima.
items.explanation: That conditional statement is the inverse of the requirement in 6-1-1.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications must be maintained before mileage-based procedures and minima are used.
items.explanation: That restates the requirement in 6-1-1.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You want to apply mileage-based separation using DME between two aircraft, but pilot communications are currently being relayed through another aircraft instead of by direct pilot/controller voice communications.
items.para_context: Under 6-1-1, use mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima only when direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.

choices.text: Do not use the mileage-based procedures unless direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.
Use the mileage-based procedures because any relay communications satisfy the paragraph.
Use the mileage-based procedures whenever radar contact exists, even without direct voice communications.
Use the mileage-based procedures only if the pilot agrees, because the communications requirement is advisory.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-1, mileage-based procedures and minima require direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-1, what is airport traffic control service based only upon?
items.explanation: The body of 3-1-1 says airport traffic control service is based only upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

choices.text: Observed or known traffic and airport conditions.
Pilot requests and unverified reports from nearby aircraft.
Only the most restrictive NOTAM in effect for the airport.

items.question_text: Under the note to 3-1-1, when operating in accordance with CFRs, it is the responsibility of the pilot to avoid collision with other aircraft.
items.explanation: That responsibility statement appears in the note to 3-1-1.

items.question_text: Why does the note to 3-1-1 say traffic information can aid pilots near terminal locations?
items.explanation: The note says the limited space around terminal locations means traffic information can aid pilots in avoiding collision.

choices.text: Because the limited space around terminal locations can make traffic information helpful in avoiding collision.
Because traffic information replaces the pilot's responsibility to see and avoid other aircraft.
Because traffic information is required only when aircraft operate outside Class B, Class C, and Class D surface areas.
Because terminal radar service areas eliminate the need for pilots to scan for traffic visually.

items.question_text: Under 5-8-1, what is not permitted when vectoring a departing aircraft on a radar SID?
items.explanation: Per 5-8-1a, concurrent use of a diverse vector area is not permitted.

choices.text: Concurrent use of a diverse vector area (DVA).
Assigning a heading within the published heading range.
Using vectors until the aircraft reaches the MVA or MIA.

items.question_text: Under 5-8-1, when a radar SID route description includes assigned-heading language with a published range of headings, assigned headings or vectors must not exceed that published range until the aircraft reaches the MVA or MIA.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-8-1b.

items.question_text: When a radar SID route description says "Fly assigned heading," "as assigned by ATC," or similar language with a published range of headings, how may headings or vectors be assigned under 5-8-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-8-1b, headings or vectors may be assigned as needed without exceeding the published range until reaching the MVA or MIA.

choices.text: As needed, without exceeding the published range until reaching the MVA or MIA.
At any heading necessary for traffic, regardless of the published range, until radar contact is terminated.
Only after canceling the SID and issuing a new route clearance.
Only after the aircraft has climbed above all minimum vectoring altitudes in the sector.

items.question_text: Under 5-13-7, coast tracks may not be used in the application of what criteria?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that coast tracks are not used in the application of either radar or nonradar separation criteria.

choices.text: Either radar or nonradar separation criteria.
Only Class A surveillance source requirements.

items.question_text: Under 5-13-7, coast tracks may be used in the application of nonradar separation criteria.
items.explanation: The paragraph prohibits use of coast tracks in the application of either radar or nonradar separation criteria.
